routes.name: North Ridge
routes.overview: Bonanza Peak is the highest point in the Wenatchee Mountains, a popular mountaineering objective. The North Ridge is the most direct approach to the summit, a straightforward Class 2 scramble over talus and low-angle rock with good routefinding.
routes.beta: From the Middle Fork Snoqualmie trailhead, approach via the alpine meadows to the north ridge base. Follow the ridge crest, staying on solid rock and stable talus. Most routefinding is straightforward and well-marked by cairns. Descend the same route.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if weather deteriorates rapidly or if adequate daylight remains for safe descent.
hazards: Talus and scree field approach — loose rock hazard, particularly on descent
Exposure on ridge sections — Grade 2 scrambling but some exposure on narrow sections
Weather development — afternoon thunderstorms common July-August
Stream crossings in approach canyon — high water in June-July
watch_out: Talus stability — disturbed talus creates rockfall hazard for parties below, move quickly through loose sections
Ridge traverse exposure — one slip on narrow sections could result in significant drop; helmets recommended
Afternoon electrical storms — summit by 1pm in summer; thunderstorm risk increases afternoon
Stream crossing — Middle Fork crossing can be dangerous high water (June-July); consider trekking poles
routes.best_season: July through October; snow lingers into June, making descent hazardous

routes.name: West Ridge Approach
routes.overview: The West Ridge approach to Bonanza Peak is longer and more secluded than the North Ridge. It involves more Class 2-3 scrambling with steeper sections and better views of the surrounding mountains. Popular for parties seeking less crowded access.
routes.beta: Approach via the west slopes through subalpine terrain. The ridge becomes steeper above 8,000 ft with Class 2-3 scrambling on red volcanic rock. Stay on stable sections and use caution on steeper pitches. Multiple descent options available.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if rock becomes loose or exposure feels uncomfortable. Several escape routes available on west face.
hazards: Volcanic rock stability — loose red/orange rock with poor friction, especially when wet
Steeper pitches — Class 2-3 sections with 30-40 degree angles, higher exposure than North Ridge
Descent route-finding — west ridge descent options are less obvious than return via ascent route
Weather risk — exposed ridge collects afternoon electrical activity
watch_out: Loose volcanic rock — red volcanic rock is notably unstable and slick when wet; test handholds carefully
Exposure increase — steeper than North Ridge with 30-50 ft exposure in places, significant falling risk
Route-finding descent — west side offers multiple descent gullies, but wrong choice adds hours; stay oriented
Weather window critical — afternoon thunderstorms more likely on exposed ridge; start early
routes.best_season: August-September for best rock stability (volcanic rock dries more slowly than granite)

routes.name: Standard Route
routes.overview: Chelan Butte is a prominent Chelan-Sawtooth Range peak offering spectacular views of Lake Chelan and the North Cascades. The standard route is an accessible Class 2 scramble via the northeast approach, gaining elevation steadily through forests then alpine meadows and talus.
routes.beta: Start from the Chelan-Sawtooth trailhead. Follow the trail to the saddle below the butte, then scramble northeast face using stable talus and rock. Routefinding is straightforward with occasional cairn markers. Descent retraces the ascent.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if rope or technical climbing appears necessary; this should remain pure Class 2.
hazards: Scree field descent — significant loose scree on east face creates rockfall hazard for descent
Exposure on upper scramble — upper sections have exposure but remain low-angle
Trail washout — approach trail subject to erosion and seasonal washout
watch_out: Descending scree — loose scree field on east face accelerates descenders; control speed with poles to prevent triggering rock
Exposure on final pitches — while still Class 2, upper sections have 50+ foot exposure; helmets recommended
Trail condition — approach trail erodes annually; may be impassable early season
routes.best_season: July-September; late June and early October possible if trails clear

routes.name: Northeast Ridge
routes.overview: Keller Mountain is a less-crowded East Cascades peak in the Stuart Range area. The Northeast Ridge provides straightforward Class 2+ access via stable granite and scrambling terrain, ideal for parties avoiding the Bonanza Peak crowds.
routes.beta: Approach via Bear's Breast Lake. Continue to the northeast ridge saddle, then follow the ridge crest. The rock is generally solid granite with good friction. A few Class 2+ sections require hands; otherwise Class 2 talus scrambling.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if Class 3 terrain is encountered; standard route should remain firmly Class 2+.
hazards: Stream crossing — Bear's Breast Lake outlet creek can be high in early season
Solid rock sections — granite is stable but may be wet with moss/lichen
Descent rockfall — exposed descent potential affects parties below
watch_out: Stream crossing hazard — creek can be thigh-deep June-July with strong current; scout for logs/rocks to cross on
Wet granite — granite faces hold moisture and become slick; use caution on any wet sections
Descending exposure — descent includes 35-40 degree sections; use deliberate technique to avoid dislodging rocks
routes.best_season: Late July through September; snow persists higher elevations through June

routes.name: South Slope Route
routes.overview: Magic Mountain, named for its visibility and prominence in the Chelan-Sawtooth region, offers a straightforward Class 2 scramble via the south slope approach. Less visited than nearby peaks, it provides good training for alpine scrambling with reliable routefinding.
routes.beta: From the lower trailhead, ascend via gentle south-facing slopes. The route follows stable talus and low-angle rock. Cairns mark the way at key junctions. The final 500 feet becomes steeper but remains Class 2 scrambling.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if the route becomes unclear or steeper than expected.
hazards: Talus field exposure — large talus boulders can shift underfoot
Southern exposure — afternoon heating on south slope increases rockfall risk later in day
Route confusion — multiple gullies on south face; poor visibility may cause wrong-turn descent
watch_out: Talus stability — some large boulders in talus field are unstable; test before putting full weight
Afternoon heating — south slope warms rapidly; afternoon sun increases rockfall from slopes above, move quickly through exposed sections by noon
Descent navigation — gullies on south face can look similar; use GPS or compass to ensure correct descent line
routes.best_season: July-September; May-June may have lingering snow on higher sections

routes.name: East Ridge (Non-Technical)
routes.overview: Colchuck Peak is primarily known for its technical alpine routes and glaciated approaches, but the East Ridge provides a non-technical Class 2-3 scrambling alternative for parties seeking to reach the summit without technical climbing. This route is less traveled than the technical options.
routes.beta: From Colchuck Lake, traverse northeast to the east ridge base. Follow the ridge crest, negotiating Class 2-3 scrambling terrain. Several sections require hands but no protection needed. The granite is generally solid with good friction. Descent retraces the ascent.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if Class 3 feels uncomfortable or if rope/protection appears necessary.
hazards: Ridge exposure — 50-100 ft drops on both sides of ridge; significant falling risk if not careful
Loose rock — some sections include loose Class 3 scrambling; rockfall hazard for any party below
Weather on ridge — exposed ridge attracts electrical storms; summiting window is critical
watch_out: Ridge exposure severe — one slip means significant drop; this is more exposed than lower-angle scrambles; proceed with caution
Loose rock sections — Class 3 sections include unstable rock; kick test all handholds and footholds
Electrical storm risk — exposed ridge; thunderstorm risk afternoon July-August; aim to summit by early afternoon
routes.best_season: Late July through September; snow persists longer on north slopes

routes.name: Standard Approach
routes.overview: Denny Mountain, located in the Enchantments area, offers a straightforward Class 2 scramble with excellent alpine views. Popular day climb from Enchantments Lake basin as part of a multi-peak Enchantments traverse.
routes.beta: From Enchantments Lake, traverse north along the alpine terrain. Ascend the northwest slope via stable talus and low-angle rock. Routefinding is straightforward. Multiple descent options available by retracing or descending east slope.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if the route becomes obscure or steeper than expected.
hazards: Alpine exposure — remote alpine terrain with long approach; weather changes rapidly
Talus field rockfall — loose talus common in Enchantments; rockfall hazard to lower parties
Stream crossings — several alpine creeks to cross on approach
watch_out: Talus rockfall — Enchantments talus fields are notably loose; move quickly and deliberately to minimize rockfall hazard
Weather isolation — long approach means weather change traps you high; start early and have clear bail strategy
Creek crossings — multiple small creeks on approach; high water June-July with snowmelt
routes.best_season: August-September (peak season conditions); July possible if spring snowmelt has receded

routes.name: West Slope Scramble
routes.overview: Cathedral Peak's primary routes involve technical rock climbing, but the west slope offers a Class 2+ scrambling alternative for parties not equipped for climbing. This approach is steeper and more exposed than other Enchantments scrambles.
routes.beta: From Enchantments Lake area, traverse to Cathedral Pass. Approach the west slope and scramble up steep talus and Class 2+ rock sections. The angle steepens above 8,100 ft. Use caution on steeper pitches. Descent retraces ascent.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if Class 3 terrain is encountered or if exposure feels unsafe.
hazards: Steep angle — 30-35 degree terrain throughout; higher angle than typical Class 2
Exposure on upper sections — significant exposure (50+ ft) on steeper pitches
Loose rock and rockfall — steep terrain creates rockfall hazard
watch_out: Steep scrambling — angles of 30-35 degrees throughout; one slip on steep sections has serious consequences
Exposure significant — 50-100 ft drops possible; helmets strongly recommended
Loose rock — steeper terrain triggers more rockfall; move deliberately and minimize loose rock
routes.best_season: Late August through September (latest season of any Enchantments peak due to exposure and elevation)

routes.name: Northeast Slope
routes.overview: Leprechaun Lake Peak offers one of the most accessible scrambles in the Enchantments area, suitable for parties with modest alpine scrambling experience. The northeast slope is a straightforward Class 2 approach ideal as a first alpine climb or as part of a larger Enchantments peak-bagging day.
routes.beta: From Leprechaun Lake, ascend the northeast slope via stable talus and low-angle rock. The route follows natural terrain with minimal routefinding difficulty. Multiple descent options available.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if the route becomes steep or obscure.
hazards: Talus field — typical Enchantments talus rockfall hazard
Alpine weather — rapid weather changes in exposed alpine terrain
Lower elevation — may have lingering snow earlier season than higher peaks
watch_out: Talus rockfall — Enchantments talus is loose and active; control descent speed to minimize rockfall
Weather rapid change — exposed alpine terrain; clear weather can deteriorate quickly; have bail plan
Snow lingers lower — may have wet, soft snow in July and early August due to lower elevation and south-facing exposure
routes.best_season: August-September

routes.name: North Cascades Foothills Route
routes.overview: Jack Mountain in the North Cascades foothills is a scenic but less-known Class 2 scramble offering good views of the North Cascades range. The south-facing slope dries earlier in season than higher north-facing peaks.
routes.beta: Approach via south-slope forest trail. Emerge into alpine terrain and follow gentle slopes northward. Ascend via stable talus. The route is well-cairned and straightforward. Descent by same route.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if the route becomes unclear or significantly steeper.
hazards: Stream crossing — approach crosses creek in forested section; high water June-July
Route confusion — alpine terrain has multiple gullies; poor visibility may cause routefinding confusion
Exposure lower than higher peaks — lower risk profile than Stuart Range or Enchantments peaks
watch_out: Stream crossing hazard — early season creek crossing can be dangerous; scout carefully
Routefinding at treeline — transition from forest to alpine can be confusing; carefully mark return route
Weather afternoon thunderstorms — foothills peaks attract afternoon electrical activity
routes.best_season: July through September

routes.name: Classic Route via Pilchuck Lake
routes.overview: Mount Pilchuck is an accessible North Cascades foothills peak offering surprisingly good views from a modest elevation gain. The trail to Pilchuck Lake transitions to a straightforward Class 2 summit scramble. Popular as a early-season training peak.
routes.beta: Well-maintained trail to Pilchuck Lake. From the lake, scramble up the Class 2 talus slope to the summit. The route is obvious and well-traveled. Watch for loose rock on descent.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if the route becomes unclear or if the scramble feels unsafe.
hazards: Loose talus descent — steep talus on descent creates rockfall hazard
Weather exposure — open ridge susceptible to afternoon electrical storms
Trail congestion — popular peak attracts crowds, especially weekends; rockfall risk from other parties
watch_out: Descending talus — use trekking poles and control speed; loose rock accelerates quickly
Afternoon thunderstorm risk — open summit exposed; afternoon electrical activity likely July-August
Crowd rockfall — popular peak; descend with care especially if other parties above you
routes.best_season: July through October; can be climbed earlier season than higher peaks due to elevation

routes.name: South Ridge Route
routes.overview: Whitehorse Mountain is a distinctive North Cascades foothills peak featuring a Class 2 scramble up the south ridge. The mountain's prominent position offers exceptional views of the North Cascades range and offers good training for alpine terrain navigation.
routes.beta: Approach via trail to the south ridge base. Scramble up the ridge via stable talus and low-angle rock. The route follows the ridge crest with good routefinding. Descent retraces the ascent.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if the route becomes significantly steeper or if rope is considered necessary.
hazards: Ridge exposure — south ridge has increasing exposure higher up (30-50 ft)
Loose rock — south-facing slope warms and potentially destabilizes talus
Descent rockfall — steep descent angle creates rockfall hazard for parties below
watch_out: Ridge exposure — south ridge exposure increases with elevation; use caution on steeper upper sections
Loose rock/talus stability — south-facing slope warms in afternoon; loose rock more problematic later day
Controlled descent — steep ridge descent requires deliberate technique; avoid dislodging rocks
routes.best_season: August-September (after south slopes dry thoroughly)

routes.name: East Slope Approach
routes.overview: Matterhorn Pass Peak, located in the Pasayten Wilderness, offers a less-visited Class 2+ scramble ideal for parties seeking solitude. The east slope approach is straightforward but slightly more technical than lower-elevation foothills peaks.
routes.beta: Approach via Pasayten trail system. Leave trail at designated point and ascend east-facing slope via Class 2+ scrambling terrain. Use cairns where available. A few rock step-overs required but route should remain Class 2+.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if Class 3 terrain is encountered or if the route becomes obscure.
hazards: Remote location — Pasayten wilderness is remote; rescue difficult in emergencies
Routefinding — less-traveled peak offers fewer cairns and less obvious route
Avalanche terrain potential — east slope aspect; check NWAC forecast for stability
Creek crossing — approach crosses stream subject to seasonal high water
watch_out: Remote wilderness — limited rescue access; self-sufficiency essential; satellite communication recommended
Routefinding challenges — sparse cairn markers; map/compass/GPS essential for navigation
Avalanche terrain — east slope could slide in unstable spring snow conditions; avoid if recent heavy snow
Stream crossing — approach creek crosses may be dangerous high water June-July
routes.best_season: August-September (lowest avalanche risk and water levels)

routes.name: Cool Glacier Non-Technical Approach
routes.overview: Glacier Peak is Washington's most remote volcano. While most routes involve technical alpine climbing or glacier travel, the Cool Glacier approach offers a Class 2-3 scrambling alternative avoiding the steepest terrain, though high elevation and commitment remain significant.
routes.beta: Approach via Sitkum Creek drainage. Ascend to Cool Glacier base using talus and low-angle rock. Climb the glacier via low-angle snowfield (Class 2-3 depending on conditions). Final summit approach via stable rock/talus.
routes.turnaround: Turn around immediately if glacier conditions appear unstable or if rope is considered necessary.
hazards: Crevasse field — Cool Glacier has extensive crevasses mid-summer; significant falling risk
Altitude — 10,500+ ft elevation presents altitude sickness risk; spend time acclimatizing
Weather — remote high peak attracts severe weather; window for safe ascent is narrow
Route hazard diversity — combines stream crossing, glaciated terrain, and high-altitude exposure
watch_out: Crevasses critical hazard — Cool Glacier develops extensive crevasse field by mid-June; serious falling risk; rope and glacier skills essential despite 'scrambling' classification
Altitude sickness — high elevation; allow proper acclimatization; descent immediately if symptoms develop
Weather window critical — window for safe ascent 1-2 weeks only late August-early September; weather deteriorates rapidly
Remote location — rescue impossible without extensive resources; be self-sufficient; communicate plans with others
routes.best_season: Late August through early September only

routes.name: West Face Scramble
routes.overview: Miners Ridge Pass Peak is a lesser-known East Cascades scramble offering good alpine terrain practice with moderate elevation gain. The west face is a straightforward Class 2 approach with solid rock and stable talus.
routes.beta: Approach via trail to Miners Ridge Pass area. Leave trail and ascend west face via stable talus and low-angle granite rock. Routefinding is straightforward. Descent retraces ascent.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if steeper than expected terrain is encountered.
hazards: Talus stability — talus field with potential loose rock
Weather — exposed face attracts afternoon weather
Stream crossing — approach includes creek crossing subject to seasonal high water
watch_out: Talus stability — test talus before putting full weight; move deliberately
Afternoon weather — exposed west face attracts afternoon thunderstorms; start early
Creek crossing — approach stream can be high water June-July
routes.best_season: July-September

routes.name: East Slope Route
routes.overview: Teneriffe Mountain is one of the lower but increasingly popular scrambles in the greater Cascade region, offering good training ground for alpine skills. The east slope is a well-traveled Class 2 route with good views and minimal exposure.
routes.beta: Trail leads to a clearing area. From there, scramble up the east-facing slope via stable talus and low-angle rock. The route is well-marked. Descent is straightforward via the same route.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if the route becomes steep or if a scramble feels unsafe.
hazards: Loose talus — typical talus field with potential loose rock
Trail exposure — final approach trail has minor exposure but manageable
Weather afternoon storms — open areas susceptible to electrical storms
watch_out: Talus loose rock — control descent speed with trekking poles
Afternoon storms — afternoon thunderstorms common; finish by early afternoon
Trail footing — approach trail has some slick sections when wet
routes.best_season: July through October

routes.name: North Ridge Route
routes.overview: Independence Peak offers a more technical scramble option in the central Cascades, ideal for parties with solid alpine terrain experience. The north ridge contains several Class 2-3 sections with significant exposure but no rock climbing required.
routes.beta: Trail leads to the base area. Scramble the north ridge crest via Class 2-3 rock and talus. Multiple hand-use sections; helmets recommended. Descent retraces ascent or descends east side depending on comfort level.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if Class 3 terrain becomes uncomfortable or if route-finding is poor.
hazards: Ridge exposure — 30-50 ft exposure on ridge crest; falling risk significant
Class 2-3 sections — technically higher than pure Class 2; hands required in several places
Loose rock — steeper sections include loose rock and rockfall hazard
Weather exposure — exposed ridge attracts storms
watch_out: Ridge exposure — 30-50 ft drops on both sides of ridge; one slip has serious consequences
Class 2-3 sections — hands required; test all holds carefully before committing weight
Loose rock prevalent — steeper sections include unstable rock; move deliberately
Weather critical — exposed ridge; afternoon thunderstorm risk; plan for early summit
routes.best_season: August-September (later summer dries rock)

routes.name: East Side Scramble
routes.overview: Crest Lake Peak offers a quick alpine scramble accessible from the Alpine Lakes Wilderness. The east side provides straightforward Class 2 terrain suitable for mid-level alpine scramblers seeking a moderate summit.
routes.beta: From Crest Lake, ascend the east-facing slope via stable talus and low-angle rock. Routefinding is straightforward. The summit offers good views of the Alpine Lakes region. Descent retraces the ascent.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if the route becomes steep or unclear.
hazards: Alpine lake approach — long hiking approach to base
Talus field — typical loose talus with rockfall hazard
Weather — alpine location susceptible to rapid weather changes
watch_out: Talus management — control descent carefully to avoid rockfall
Weather rapid change — alpine terrain; weather deteriorates quickly; have clear bail plan
Route via lakes — navigation in alpine lake basins; map/compass essential
routes.best_season: August-September

routes.name: Northeast Ridge Route
routes.overview: Source Lake Peak is an accessible Chelan-Sawtooth peak offering good training for alpine scrambling. The northeast ridge approach is straightforward Class 2 with stable rock and well-defined terrain.
routes.beta: Approach via Source Lake trail system. Ascend the northeast ridge via stable talus and low-angle rock. Cairns mark key junctions. Descent retraces the ascent or can descend east side if conditions permit.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if the route becomes steep or if routefinding becomes unclear.
hazards: Ridge exposure — 30-40 ft exposure on upper ridge sections
Talus field — typical loose talus with rockfall hazard
Weather — afternoon thunderstorm risk common
watch_out: Ridge exposure — 30-40 ft drops on upper ridge; use caution on narrow sections
Talus loose rock — control descent to minimize rockfall for parties below
Afternoon storms — thunderstorm risk afternoon; plan to summit by early afternoon
routes.best_season: July-September

routes.name: West Slope Scramble
routes.overview: Snoqualmie Peak is an accessible Central Cascades objective offering good views of the Interstate 90 corridor and surrounding peaks. The west slope approach is a straightforward Class 2 scramble suitable for mid-level alpine scramblers.
routes.beta: Approach via established trail to the saddle west of peak. Ascend the west-facing slope via stable talus and low-angle rock. Routefinding is generally straightforward with occasional cairns. Descent retraces ascent.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if the route becomes unclear or steeper than expected.
hazards: Trail exposure — approach trail has minor exposure in places
Talus field — typical loose talus with rockfall potential
Weather afternoon storms — exposed peak attracts afternoon electrical activity
watch_out: Trail footing — approach trail has minor exposure; watch for loose rocks
Talus descent — control speed with trekking poles; loose talus accelerates quickly
Afternoon thunderstorm risk — afternoon electrical activity; finish summit by early afternoon
routes.best_season: July through October

routes.name: South Buttress
watch_out: Loose rock on summit pyramid (3/4-class summit block); expect rockfall from above when congested
Late-season glacier hardness on Upper Curtis Glacier; hard ice requires crampons; aluminum adequate but conditions tenuous
Serac/avalanche debris fields in Shuksan Arm approach; spring/early summer activity; rain can trigger additional slides
Route-finding complexity through tortuous chimney system; wrong gully has moss/lichen-covered rock; easy to lose route in early season snow
Crevasse/bergschrund exposure on White Salmon Glacier; temporary bridges deteriorate late season; open crevasses by August

routes.name: Southeast Buttress
watch_out: Moderate technical scrambling on good rock (southeast facing)
Exposed sections with 40-50 foot drops to either side
Route-finding moderately complex in upper sections
Best August-September before freeze-thaw hazards peak

routes.name: North Ridge
watch_out: North Ridge Basin avalanche zone - April-May freeze-thaw activity
Moderate exposure and mixed terrain
Optimal climbing window: August-September
Longer hours in early season due to snow coverage

routes.name: Cauthorn-Wilson Couloir
watch_out: HIGH HAZARD - Documented rappel failure incident (April 28, 2024: girth hitch failure, ~400 foot fall, broken leg)
Steep water ice sections requiring technical ice climbing
Avalanche hazard in upper couloir sections
Rappel setup critical - verify anchors and rappel setup redundancy
Verify all rappel hardware: girth hitches have known failure modes

routes.name: Northeast Face
watch_out: Less frequented route with faded trail markings
Route-finding challenging in some sections
High alpine exposure at 8,050 ft
35-50 degree daytime temperatures; 20-30 mph sustained wind
Lightning risk on exposed ridge

deployment_instructions.track_1: Insert routes directly into routes table
deployment_instructions.track_2: Update existing routes watch_out field via name-based matching
deployment_instructions.track_3: Verify insertion counts and coverage metrics
